coredata.dc:title: High-performance supercapacitors based on porous activated carbon derived from carbon dots by directly pyrolyzing industrial glucose
coredata.dc:creator: Pu, Jianlin
Wang, Lanxin
Huang, Jia
Chen, Qiuhong
Jin, Yanzi
Chen, Jiucun
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), a new member of the family of carbon materials, can be potentially used in electronic devices. In this study, porous activated carbon derived from CDs (ACD) was synthesized using industrial glucose as a carbon source via a direct pyrolysis, followed by KOH activation with a low mass ratio. Owing to the co-contribution of CDs and double-layer capacitance, the obtained ACD exhibited a maximum specific capacitance of 350 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and a superior rate capability (248 F g−1 even at 20 A g−1) when the mass ratio of KOH to carbon was 0.75. Furthermore, the symmetric supercapacitor using the optimized ACD as electrodes exhibited a remarkable energy density of 7.3 Wh Kg−1 at a power density of 251.7 W Kg−1 and an unexceptionable cycling stability with only 3 % capacitance decay over 10,000 charge-discharge cycles at 2 A g−1. Thus, the low cost, ease of preparation, and suitable electrochemical performance endow CDs-derived porous activated carbon with promising application in the supercapacitor industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Porous activated carbon
Industrial glucose
Suitable electrochemical performance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and chemical structure of ACD

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The FESEM images of (a) ACD-0, (b) ACD-0.25, (c) ACD-0.5, (d) ACD-0.75, (e) ACD-1, and the TEM images of (f) molten liquid, (g) ACD precursor, (h) ACD-0.75.
(a) The XRD patterns and (b) the Raman spectra of ACD.
(a) The N2 adsorption and desorption curves and (b) the pore size distribution curves of ACD.
The XPS spectra of ACD-0.75. (a) Full spectrum, (b) C1s spectrum, (c) O1s spectrum.

(a) The CV curves (20 mV s−1), (b) the GCD curves (1 A g−1), (c) specific capacitance versus current density of ACD, and (d) the cycling performance of ACD-0.75 at current density of 5 A g−1 in a three-electrode system (the illustration showed the GCD curves after the 1st and 10000th charge-discharge cycles).
(a) The CV curves of ACD-0.75 at different scanning rates and (b) the GCD curves of ACD-0.75 at different current densities (in a three-electrode system).

(a) The CV curves of ACD-0.75 at different scanning rates, (b) the GCD curves of ACD-0.75 at different current densities, (c) the cycling performance and Coulomb efficiency of ACD-0.75 at 2 A g−1, and (d) the Nyquist curve of ACD-0.75 (in a two-electrode system).
Summary of Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) characteristics of ACD.
EIS fitting parameters of the electrodes based on the corresponding proposed equivalent circuit.

head.ce:title: High-performance supercapacitors based on porous activated carbon derived from carbon dots by directly pyrolyzing industrial glucose

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory for Advanced Material & Technologies of Clean Energies, School of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory for Advanced Material & Technologies of Clean Energies

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Key Laboratory for Advanced Material & Technologies of Clean Energies, School of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sichuan Vocational College of Chemical Technology, LuZhou 646000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sichuan Vocational College of Chemical Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Sichuan Vocational College of Chemical Technology, LuZhou 646000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), a new member of the family of carbon materials, can be potentially used in electronic devices. In this study, porous activated carbon derived from CDs (ACD) was synthesized using industrial glucose as a carbon source via a direct pyrolysis, followed by KOH activation with a low mass ratio. Owing to the co-contribution of CDs and double-layer capacitance, the obtained ACD exhibited a maximum specific capacitance of 350 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and a superior rate capability (248 F g−1 even at 20 A g−1) when the mass ratio of KOH to carbon was 0.75. Furthermore, the symmetric supercapacitor using the optimized ACD as electrodes exhibited a remarkable energy density of 7.3 Wh Kg−1 at a power density of 251.7 W Kg−1 and an unexceptionable cycling stability with only 3 % capacitance decay over 10,000 charge-discharge cycles at 2 A g−1. Thus, the low cost, ease of preparation, and suitable electrochemical performance endow CDs-derived porous activated carbon with promising application in the supercapacitor industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors are among the most high-potential energy storage devices, which are characterized by rapid charge and discharge, high safety, long-cycle life, and high power density [1]. Electrode materials are the key to the performance of supercapacitors. The study of electrode materials with excellent performance bears significance for the development of supercapacitors [2]. Carbon materials have been widely used as electrode materials for supercapacitors due to their outstanding chemical stability, high specific surface area, low cost, good electrical conductivity, and abundant sources [3–6]. The research on carbon-based electrode materials for supercapacitors mainly concentrates on the preparation of porous activated carbon materials with adjustable surface functional groups, low internal resistance and high specific surface area.

Glucose, the most widely distributed monosaccharide in nature, has been considered one of the most promising carbon sources for porous carbon electrode materials because of its low cost, high activity, and nontoxicity [7]. Li et al. took glucose as the precursor and activated it with KOH to form pores [8]. When the mass ratio of KOH to activated carbon was 3, the specific capacitance of sample prepared was the highest, reaching 207 F g−1 (at a current density of 0.5 A g−1). Wang et al. prepared nitrogen-doped sheet of carbon using glucose and melamine as raw materials, and then activated the products with KOH (alkali/carbon ratio was 2) [9]. At a current density of 0.5 A g−1, the specific capacitance of the final product was 312 F g−1. Fu et al. hydrothermally combined glucose and carbon nanotubes, and activated the composite product with ZnCl2 as the activator (the mass ratio of ZnCl2 to the composite product was 4), and successfully prepared the porous carbon with a specific capacitance of 210 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1 [10]. Although the route of preparing porous carbon with glucose as the precursor is increasingly mature, it still has bottlenecks of low specific capacitance and consumption of a lot of activators (such as expensive KOH). Doping or recombination has been shown to be an effective way to improve the electrochemical properties of porous carbon. However, their preparation methods and processes are complex, involving more kinds of chemical raw materials and chemical reaction steps. In addition, most porous activated carbon materials using glucose as the carbon source was fabricated via a hydrothermal carbonization or direct pyrolysis with the template-assisted method, by using inorganic matrices (e.g., SiO2, Zn(NO3)2, ZnCl2), or by using polymers (e.g., polyvinyl pyrrolidone) to create a hierarchical pore structure [11]. However, these templates need to be removed using strong acids, resulting in environmental pollution and high costs [12]. Moreover, a multistep process is also required.

Owing to good dispersibility, abundant surface groups, and low production cost, carbon dots (CDs), have become the focus of research on energy storage materials [13–18]. Previous studies have shown that CDs can promote an increase in the effective contact surface between an electrolyte and an electrode, which can help improve the specific capacity, rate performance, and cycle stability of the material [19,20]. In addition, the involvement of CDs in the formation of larger nanostructures facilitates the formation of more ion channels, which is conducive to improving the ion transport rate [21]. Therefore, all these advantages can ensure that electrode materials based on CDs exhibit potential for practical applications.

Prompted by the aforementioned considerations, a facile method was proposed in this study to synthesize porous activated carbon derived from carbon dots (ACD) with superior electrochemical properties. The ACD precursor was first prepared using industrial glucose as the carbon source by direct pyrolysis. The ACD was then produced with a low KOH content. Consequently, the ACD exhibited good electrochemical performance when employed as an electrode material for supercapacitors.

The industrial glucose used in the experiments was purchase from Suzhou Jubaiyi Chemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Suzhou, China). Other chemicals were supplied by Aladdin Chemicals Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used without further purification.

Approximately 10 g of industrial glucose was poured into a 250 mL beaker, directly heated on a thermostatic heating table and stirred until it was carbonized to form a dark brown bulk. The heating temperature was maintained at 200 °C for 15 min. After natural cooling, it was ground into powder to obtain the ACD precursor. KOH and 1 g of the obtained ACD precursor were dispersed in 10 mL of deionized water at different mass ratios (0:1, 0.25:1, 0.5:1, and 0.75:1), and then stirred until uniform suspension was obtained. It was put into a drying oven for full drying (at 80 °C for 24 h). The dried product was put into a horizontal tube furnace and heated to 800 °C at a rate of 5 °C min−1. The heated product was kept in an argon atmosphere for 2 h and cooled naturally to room temperature. It was ultimately washed to neutral with 0.3 % dilute hydrochloric acid solution and water and then dried to obtain ACD. The ACD prepared using KOH in different proportions were labeled as ACD-0, ACD-0.25, ACD-0.5, ACD-0.75, and ACD-1.

The ACD was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL 2100), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, JSM-7800F), X-ray diffraction (XRD, Shimadzu XRD-7000), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250xi), Autosorb-1 (Quantachrome Instruments), Jobin-Yvon HR 800 spectrometer.

ACD, conductive agent Super P and binder polytetrafluoroethylene solution (solid content 60 wt%) were dispersed into anhydrous ethanol at a mass ratio of 8:1:1 and fully ground to obtain a viscous slurry. Then the slurry is evenly scraped onto a square nickel foam collector with a side length of 1 cm (the mass loading of electrode was 1.5–3 mg cm−2). After drying overnight at 80 °C, it was taken out and then put on the powder tablet press for 15 s at the pressure of 2 MPa to prepare the working electrode. Electrochemical workstation (Chi 760E, Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co. Ltd., China) was used to analyze its electrochemical performance in two and three electrode system. Using a standard three electrode system, platinum foil and Hg/HgO electrode were used as the counter and reference electrode in 3 M KOH aqueous solution, respectively. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted at the open circuit voltage (VOC) over a frequency range of 105–10−2 Hz. As for the assembled symmetric supercapacitor, two electrodes with the same size and quality of active materials were assembled with a filter paper separator (the thickness of electrode was approximately 0.5 mm, the mass loading of electrode was 2–3 mg cm−2, and the density of electrode was 40–60 mg cm−3), and the electrochemical tests were measured in 3 M KOH aqueous solution.

The specific capacitance of ACD was calculated from the charge/discharge curves (Fig. 5b) by the following Eq. (1):
(1)
C =
I × △ t
/
m × △ V
where C (F g−1) is specific capacitance based on the mass of the active carbons, I (A) and ∆t are the constant charge/discharge current and discharge time measured in seconds, meanwhile m (g) and ∆V are the mass of active materials and potential window (V).

The specific capacitance for a two-electrode symmetric supercapacitor device can be calculated from the GCD curves (Fig. 8b) by using the following Eq. (2):
(2)
C device
= 2
I × △ t
/
m × △ V

The energy density (E) and power density (P) of a two-electrode device were calculated by using the following Eqs. (3) and (4):
(3)
E =
C device
× △
V 2
/
8 × 3.6
(4)
P =
E × 3600
/ △ t

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and chemical structure of ACD
ce:section.ce:para: With industrial glucose as the carbon source, ACD was synthesized by direct pyrolysis (Scheme 1 ).

The morphology and microstructure of ACD were observed by FESEM and TEM. Pore formation of carbon blocks was conducted by etching and dehydration reaction with KOH at 800 °C. As shown in Fig. 1a–d, with an increase in the amount of KOH added, the number of pores on carbon blocks gradually increased, the uniformity of pore size was increased, the void became more flawless, and no damage to the structure of carbon blocks occurred. However, as shown in Fig. 1e, when KOH was excessive, the skeletal structure of carbon blocks would be destroyed, leading to the formation of large pores and the collapse of pores [22]. Consequently, the gap utilization rate and the electrochemical performance of ACD deteriorated.

During heating at 200 °C, industrial glucose powders were melted and then carbonized. After the industrial glucose powder completely melted at 200 °C, we obtained a large number of CDs by dialysis and freeze-drying (the model of the freeze-dryer system: CS100-9 Pro) of the molten liquid of glucose (Fig. 1f). The TEM image of CDs in Fig. 1f showed almost spherical shaped particles, with size ranging from of 5 to 30 nm. The uneven particle size distribution of the prepared CDs might be caused by the uneven direct heating of the industrial glucose powder in the beaker. With continuous heating of the molten liquid, and in this high temperature heat melting process, some of the CDs were sintered together to form carbon blocks, which also confirmed that ACD precursor was derived by further carbonization of CDs and ACD was synthesized from industrial glucose through CDs. As shown in Fig. 1g, dispersed CDs were visible on the surface of the ACD precursor. When the precursor was activated by KOH, the porous structure of ACD was still loaded with CDs (Fig. 1h), which had the characteristics of rapid electron transfer and high specific surface area, thus improving the electrochemical energy storage of ACD.

The crystal structure of ACD was analyzed by XRD. Fig. 2a showed the similar XRD patterns of ACD. All XRD curves have two typical diffraction peaks (around 24° and 43°) which are the same as those of graphitic carbon, representing the (002) crystal plane and the (100) crystal plane, respectively [23]. The appearance of the graphite accumulation of (002) indicated that ACD was the disordered graphitized structure. With a gradual increase in the KOH activation ratio, the peak of (002) evidently shifted to a lower angle, indicating that the layer spacing of carbon gradually increased, which was conducive to the embedding and diffusion of ions. However, when the KOH ratio was too large, the peak of (002) moved to a higher angle, which was attributable to the structural collapse of ACD, leading to the reduction of layer spacing (on the basis of the FESEM images, excessive KOH would cause the collapse of ACD structure). To further analyze the degree of graphitization of ACD, the Raman spectra of ACD were presented in Fig. 2b. Two apparent peaks were observed at 1341 and 1596 cm−1, namely, the D and G peaks, respectively. The D peak in the figure corresponded to the defective and irregularly arranged vibration peaks of carbon, and the G peak corresponded to the vibration peaks of sp2 hybridization in the crystal structure of carbon [24]. Generally, the degree of graphitization of carbon materials was measured by the intensity ratio of the D peak and G peak (ID/IG). The higher the ID/IG value, the lower the degree of graphitization [24]. The ID/IG of ACD-0, ACD-0.25, ACD-0.5, ACD-0.75 and ACD-1 was calculated to be 0.95, 0.99, 0.98, 0.97, and 1.01, respectively. With the addition of KOH, the ID/IG value of ACD slightly increased, indicating that KOH led to the decrease of ACD order degree and graphitization degree. This is due to KOH activated ACD precursor to make pores, endowing ACD with more pore structures, resulting in defects in the carbon structure.

The effect of different KOH additions on the specific surface area and pore structure of ACD can be explained by the N2 adsorption and desorption test. Fig. 3a showed the N2 adsorption and desorption curves of ACD. All ACD samples, except for ACD-0, mainly exhibited the characteristics of the I/IV curve. The greater the amount of KOH added, the higher the adsorption platform of ACD with linear growth in the P/P0 < 0.1 region. When 0.75 g of KOH was added, the adsorption platform of ACD reached the highest value, indicating that an appropriate amount of KOH was conducive to the formation of the microporous structure of ACD and could increase its specific surface area (ACD-0.75 had the largest specific surface area, which was 1246 m2 g−1). In the 0.4 < P/P0 < 0.9 region, ACD-0.5, ACD-0.75, and ACD-1 had obvious H4-type hysteresis loops, indicating that these materials also contained more mesopores [25]. The pore size distribution curves of ACD were shown in Fig. 3b. The pore size distribution was mainly concentrated in the 1.8–4.2 nm range. Notably, the pore size distribution curves of ACD were relatively smooth, that is, the pore size distribution was relatively uniform, which also confirmed that CDs can render the ACD precursor uniformly activated from the inside out. Summary of Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) characteristics of ACD such as specific surface area (SBET), average pore diameter (Dp), and total pore volume (Vtotal) were listed in Table 1 .

The chemical composition of ACD was analyzed by XPS. As can be seen from the XPS full-scan spectrum (Fig. 4a), ACD-0.75 had two apparent absorption peaks at 284.2 and 532.1 eV, which represented the characteristic peaks of C1s and O1s, respectively, indicating that ACD-0.75 contained carbon and oxygen elements. Fig. 4b presented the peak splitting results for C1s. The peak at 283.2 eV corresponded to CC in the form of sp2 hybridization [26]. The peak at 284.4 eV was ascribed to CC or CO, and the peak at 286.5 eV corresponded to CO or –COOH [27]. The band of O1s could be divided into three peaks (Fig. 4c), matching CO (530.4 eV), -COOH (532.5 eV), and CO (534.3 eV) [28]. These oxygen-containing functional groups on the ACD-0.75 surface could improve the surface wettability to a certain extent, facilitating the entry of electrolyte ions to the interior of the pores, improving the utilization of micropores and mesopores.

The electrochemical properties of ACD were compared in a three-electrode system (3 M KOH). Fig. 5a presented the CV curves of ACD prepared with different amounts of KOH added at the same scanning rate (20 mV s−1). The figure illustrated that the CV curves of ACD showed a potential window with a rectangular shape overall, indicating the typical double-layer capacitance characteristics of the materials. Moreover, no apparent REDOX peak was observed in the CV curves, indicating that the capacitance of the supercapacitor using ACD as the electrode material was mainly provided by the double layer. The specific capacitance of ACD was proportional to the area enclosed by the CV curves. As shown in the figure, with an increase in the KOH activation ratio, ACD-0.75 had a larger envelop area, compared with other samples under the same sweep speed, suggesting a higher capacitance. The main reason was that ACD-0.75 had the largest specific surface area, the most uniform pore structure, and the most appropriate voidage, and ACD-0.75 was the most conducive to ion exchange in the electrolyte. Notably, in general hole-making, a substantial amount of KOH was involved. However, in the current study, when the precursor was fully activated to achieve the optimal capacitance performance, the amount of KOH used was considerably small (the mass ratio of the precursor and KOH was 0.75), greatly reducing the cost of KOH. When the ratio of the precursor to KOH reached 1:1, the area of the CV curve decreased. The reason was that the excessively high ratio of KOH led to the collapse and crushing of a large number of pore structures, thus reducing the specific surface area and pore volume significantly. Under the activation of an appropriate amount of KOH, the CV curves of activated products had a larger response current (i.e., a corresponding increase in specific capacitance). The specific capacitance of ACD-0.75 reached the maximum, indicating that the pore structure of the products was more completely developed after the activation of an appropriate amount of KOH. This finding was consistent with the results of the N2 adsorption and desorption test.

Fig. 5b presented the GCD curves of ACD at the same current density (1 A g−1). All resembled an isosceles triangle, indicating not only the capacitance behavior of a double layer, but also a good Coulomb efficiency. A slight deviation from the linearity of the GCD curves during discharge demonstrated the existence of a small amount of pseudocapacitance. As calculated, the mass specific capacitances of ACD-0, ACD-0.25, ACD-0.5, ACD-0.75, and ACD-1 were 45, 230, 286, 350, and 200 F g −1 (at 1 A g−1), respectively, which were consistent with the results obtained by the CV curves. Notably, the specific surface area of ACD itself was not highly prominent (the high specific surface area of the biomass-activated carbon could reach about 3000 m2 g−1 [29]) but could show good capacitance performance, which was inseparable from the promotive effect of CDs with high specific surface area and a fast electron transfer rate. The rate performance was also one of the key performance parameters of supercapacitors, reflecting the attenuation of material specific capacitance at different current densities, particularly at high current densities. No significant differences in the rate performance of the five materials were observed (Fig. 5c). When the current density was 20 A g−1, the specific capacitance of ACD-0.75 was maintained at 248 F g−1. The specific capacitance of material attenuated less at a high current density, indicating that it exhibited good rate performance and excellent electrochemical performance. Cyclic stability was an extremely critical factor to evaluate the practical application of ACD, determining the service life and future application prospect of ACD. Fig. 5d showed the cyclic test results of ACD-0.75 after 10,000 repeated charge-discharge cycles at 5 A g−1. In general, as the number of cycles increased, the specific capacitance exhibited a downward trend. The possible reason was that some holes in the material were blocked after multiple charges and discharges, resulting in a decrease in the utilization rate of the specific surface area, leading to capacitance reduction with an increase in the number of cycles. If the magnitude of capacitance attenuation was smaller, the cycle stability was higher, and the service life was longer. As determined from the cyclic test results in Fig. 5d, after 10,000 charge-discharge cycles, the capacitance retention rate of ACD-0.75 remained as high as 92 % (at 5 A g−1), indicating that ACD-0.75 had high cyclic stability and long service life.

To further explore the internal electrochemical characteristics of ACD, we also performed the AC impedance test. The Nyquist curves of ACD were shown in Fig. 6 in which the slope of ACD-0 was lower than that of the other four materials in the low-frequency region, indicating that the activation of KOH could render the pore structure of ACD more developed [30]. Consequently, the electrolyte ions had a higher diffusion rate on the surface of the electrode material [31]. ACD-0.75 had the highest slope, suggesting that it had the most developed pore structure, so as to achieve the best capacitance characteristics [32]. In the high-frequency region, ACD-0 had the smallest semicircle diameter, whereas ACD-1 had the largest one. Moreover, the semicircle diameter of the other three materials slightly varied. This difference showed that the charge transfer resistance of ACD slightly increased with the addition of KOH, which might be due to a decrease in the degree of graphitization of ACD, thus increasing its resistance. The results were consistent with those of the Raman test. The intersection of the Z' axis and the semicircle located in the high-frequency region reflected the equivalent series resistance (ESR) of the electrode, which was related to the resistance of the electrolyte. A considerably high ESR would cause a waste of electrical energy and damage to the supercapacitor itself. Fig. 6 showed that all materials had a low ESR of about 0.6 Ω. In addition, Table 2 was EIS fitting parameters of the electrodes based on the corresponding proposed equivalent circuit.

The electrochemical properties of ACD-0.75 were evaluated separately in a three-electrode system (3 M KOH). As can be seen from the CV curves (Fig. 7a), when the scanning rate was increased to 200 mV s−1, the CV curves could still maintain a nearly rectangular shape, demonstrating the good magnification performance of ACD-0.75. Similarly, its GCD curves (Fig. 7b) were still a symmetrical triangle at a high current density of up to 20 A g−1, ensuring the good electrochemical performance of ACD-0.75 during rapid charge and discharge.

Finally, to further investigate the device performance of ACD-0.75, its electrochemical performance was tested in a two-electrode system. Fig. 8a showed the CV curves of ACD-0.75. At different scanning rates (10–200 mV s−1), the CV curves resembled a rectangle, indicating that the specific capacitance generated by ACD-0.75 mainly came from the double-layer reaction. In addition, even at a high scanning rate (200 mV s−1), the CV curves of ACD-0.75 barely changed their shape, meaning that ACD-0.75 had the advantage of fast charge transfer rate. Fig. 8b presented the GCD curves of ACD-0.75. In the figure, the specific capacitance of ACD-0.75 was still 210 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 in the two-electrode system. ACD-0.75 exhibited a remarkable energy density of 7.3 Wh Kg−1 at a power density of 251.7 W Kg−1, indicating a good electrochemical performance. At a high current density (20 A g−1), the specific capacitance of ACD-0.75 could still be maintained at 180 F g−1, showing excellent rate performance. The cycling stability of ACD-0.75 in the two-electrode system was shown in Fig. 8c. After 10,000 cycles, about 97 % of the capacity of ACD-0.75 was retained (at 2 A g−1), and the Coulomb efficiency barely changed. Fig. 8d presented the Nyquist curve of ACD-0.75. In the low-frequency region, the slope of the straight line was high, and only a slight deviation from the straight line perpendicular to the Z' axis was observed, indicating the high ionic diffusion capacity of the material. The small diameter of the semicircle located in the high-frequency region indicated that the material exhibited good electrical conductivity and excellent charge transfer capacity. Thus, ACD-0.75 had broad prospects in practical application.

We successfully developed a facile, scalable method to fabricate ACD electrode materials by using industrial glucose as a carbon source. ACD presented a maximum specific capacitance of 350 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 when the mass ratio of KOH to carbon was 0.75. Moreover, ACD exhibited good capacitance performance (248 F g−1 at 20 A g−1) and cycling stability (97 % after 10,000 charge-discharge cycles). The symmetric supercapacitor using the optimized ACD as electrodes possessed remarkable energy density equal to 7.3 Wh Kg−1 at a power density of 251.7 W Kg−1. The proposed strategy provides a facile method for fabricating the porous carbon materials derived from CDs using industrial glucose as a carbon source at a large scale, which is important to their practical applications in supercapacitors, filters, and batteries.

Jianlin Pu: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Methodology, Software. Lanxin Wang: Data curation, Methodology, Validation. Jia Huang: Data curation, Validation. Qiuhong Chen: Software, Validation. Yanzi Jin: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Jiucun Chen: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Transformative Project for Excellent Scientific and Technological Achievements in University (KJZH17108).

Chem. Soc. Rev.

J. Mater. Chem. A

Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.

J. Am. Chem. Soc.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen and oxygen co-doped glucose-based carbon materials with enhanced electrochemical performances as supercapacitors
